parts.content: State the condition for rotational equilibrium.
parts.markscheme:
- State the condition: The sum of the torques (or moments) acting on the body must be zero ($\sum \tau = 0$) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Recall rotational equilibrium
steps.content:
For an object to be in translational equilibrium, the net force must be zero. Similarly, for an object to be in **rotational equilibrium**, it must have no tendency to rotate (or its angular velocity must remain constant).

This concept is explored in **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**, which looks at how forces cause rotation through a quantity called torque (or moment).

steps.title: State the condition
steps.content:
The fundamental condition for rotational equilibrium is that the **resultant torque** (or sum of moments) acting on the body must be zero.

Mathematically, we write this as:
$$\sum \tau = 0$$

This ensures that there is no net turning effect on the rod.

highlights.text: The sum of the torques (or moments) acting on the body must be zero (\sum \tau = 0)

steps.title: Consider clockwise/anti-clockwise moments
steps.content:
Another way to state this condition is that the sum of the **clockwise moments** must equal the sum of the **counter-clockwise moments** about any pivot point.

In this specific problem, if there were a force holding the rod up, its clockwise effect would have to perfectly balance the counter-clockwise effect of the $3.0\text{ kg}$ mass to keep the rod in equilibrium.

parts.content: Calculate the torque due to the hanging mass about the pivot.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into torque formula: $\tau = Fr = (3.0 \cdot 9.81) \cdot 1.2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final value with unit: $\tau = 35.3\ \text{N m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the downward force
steps.content:
The torque is created by the weight of the $3.0\ \text{kg}$ mass. First, we need to calculate this force using Newton's Second Law from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet.

Using the formula for weight ($W=mg$):
$$\begin{aligned} F_g &= m \cdot g \\ F_g &= 3.0\ \text{kg} \times 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2} \\ F_g &= 29.4\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine perpendicular distance
steps.content:
Torque is defined as the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the pivot. 

Because the rod is **held horizontally** and the force of gravity acts vertically downwards, the entire length of the rod is the perpendicular distance ($r_{\perp}$).

$$r_{\perp} = 1.2\ \text{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the torque
steps.content:
Now, we multiply the force by the perpendicular distance to find the torque ($\tau$). This concept is covered in **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

$$\begin{aligned} \tau &= F \times r_{\perp} \\ \tau &= 29.4\ \text{N} \times 1.2\ \text{m} \\ \tau &= 35.28\ \text{N m} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to an appropriate number of significant figures (usually 2 or 3 in IB Physics), we get **$35.3\ \text{N m}$**.

gdc.instructions: Multiply the mass, gravity, and rod length.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication of the weight and the distance.

desmos.instructions: Type the product of the values.

calculator.description: Calculating the final torque value.

steps.title: Understanding the marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you would earn marks as follows:
*   **M1**: For substituting the correct values ($3.0 \times 9.8$ or $9.81$) and the distance ($1.2$) into a torque expression.
*   **A1**: For the correct final answer of $35.3\ \text{N m}$ with the proper units.

- Conclude the motion: The rod will rotate in the direction of the greater torque (clockwise). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the clockwise torque
steps.content:
First, we need to calculate the torque produced by the $3.0\text{ kg}$ mass hanging at the end of the rod. This force acts downward at a distance of $1.2\text{ m}$ from the pivot, creating a clockwise torque.

Using the gravitational constant from the data booklet:
$$g = 9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$$

The weight (force) is:
$$W = mg = 3.0 \times 9.8 = 29.4\text{ N}$$

The clockwise torque ($\tau_{cw}$) is calculated as:
$$\tau_{cw} = \text{Force} \times \text{distance} = 29.4 \times 1.2 = 35.28\text{ N m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the counter-clockwise torque
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the torque from the new upward force. The question states this force is equal to the weight ($29.4\text{ N}$) and is applied at the midpoint ($0.6\text{ m}$) of the rod. This force acts upward, creating a counter-clockwise torque.

The counter-clockwise torque ($\tau_{ccw}$) is:
$$\tau_{ccw} = \text{Force} \times \text{distance} = 29.4 \times 0.6 = 17.64\text{ N m}$$

highlights.text: vertical upward force of magnitude equal to the weight of the hanging mass

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content: We can use a calculator to quickly compare these two values and determine the net effect on the rod.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression for net torque directly: (3 * 9.8 * 1.2) - (3 * 9.8 * 0.6)

ti84.instructions: Calculate the weight: 3.0 * 9.8 [ENTER]. Then calculate clockwise torque: 29.4 * 1.2 [ENTER]. Then calculate counter-clockwise torque: 29.4 * 0.6 [ENTER].

desmos.expression: W = 3.0 * 9.8; T_cw = W * 1.2; T_ccw = W * 0.6; T_net = T_cw - T_ccw
desmos.instructions: Define the weight and distances, then calculate the difference.

calculator.description: Calculate and compare both torques to find the net torque.

steps.title: Compare torques and conclude
steps.content:
To determine the motion, we compare the two torques. Since $\tau_{cw} = 35.28\text{ N m}$ is greater than $\tau_{ccw} = 17.64\text{ N m}$, the net torque is not zero.

$$\tau_{net} = 35.28 - 17.64 = 17.64\text{ N m} \text{ (clockwise)}$$

Because there is a resultant clockwise torque, the rod will rotate in the clockwise direction. This explanation earns marks for calculating the new torque and correctly concluding the direction of motion based on the comparison.

steps.title: Identify the forces
steps.content:
To understand the motion, we first identify the two opposing forces acting on the rod:
1. The **weight** ($W$) of the hanging mass acting downwards at the free end.
2. The **upward force** ($F_{up}$) applied at the midpoint.

The question specifies that the magnitude of the upward force is equal to the weight of the mass.

highlights.text: vertical upward force of magnitude equal to the weight of the hanging mass

steps.title: Compare force magnitudes
steps.content:
Since the upward force is equal to the weight of the $3.0\text{ kg}$ mass, we have:

$$F_{up} = W = mg$$

Both forces are equal in magnitude. In any torque analysis, if the forces are equal, the resulting torque depends entirely on the **lever arm** (the perpendicular distance from the pivot).

steps.title: Compare lever arms
steps.content:
Now we compare the distances from the pivot (lever arms):
- The hanging mass is at the **free end**, which is the full length of the rod ($d_W = 1.2\text{ m}$).
- The upward force is at the **midpoint** ($d_{up} = 0.6\text{ m}$).

The lever arm for the weight is twice as long as the lever arm for the upward force.

steps.title: Analyze torque proportionality
steps.content:
Torque ($\tau$) is defined as force multiplied by the lever arm ($\tau = F \times d$). 

Because the forces are equal ($F_{up} = W$), the torque is directly proportional to the distance from the pivot. Since the weight acts at a **greater distance** than the upward force ($1.2\text{ m} > 0.6\text{ m}$), it must generate a **larger torque**.

$$\tau_{weight} > \tau_{upward}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation screen, compute the weight torque and the upward force torque.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the weight: 3.0 * 9.8. Then calculate the torques: (Ans) * 1.2 and (Ans) * 0.6.

desmos.instructions: Enter the following to see the comparison of the two torques.

calculator.description: Calculate and compare the numerical values of the torques using $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

steps.title: Conclude the motion
steps.content:
The torque from the hanging mass acts to rotate the rod **clockwise**, while the torque from the upward force acts **counter-clockwise**. 

Since the clockwise torque is greater, the net torque is non-zero and directed clockwise. Therefore, the rod will **rotate in the clockwise direction**.

parts.content: Calculate the skater’s final angular velocity.
parts.markscheme:
Angular momentum is conserved because there is no external torque:
$I_i \omega_i = I_f \omega_f$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\omega_f = \frac{I_i \omega_i}{I_f} = \frac{4.0 \times 2.0}{1.5} = 5.33\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
First, we list the values provided in the problem for the initial and final states of the figure skater. This problem involves rotational motion, which is covered in **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

*   Initial angular speed ($\omega_i$): $2.0\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
*   Initial moment of inertia ($I_i$): $4.0\ \text{kg m}^2$
*   Final moment of inertia ($I_f$): $1.5\ \text{kg m}^2$

highlights.text: moment of inertia of 4.0 kg m²

steps.title: Conservation of Angular Momentum
steps.content:
When the skater pulls her arms in, no external torque is acting on her. Therefore, her angular momentum is conserved. We use the principle of conservation of angular momentum:

$$I_i \omega_i = I_f \omega_f$$

steps.title: Solve for final velocity
steps.content:
To find the final angular velocity ($\omega_f$), we rearrange the equation and substitute our known values:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega_f &= \frac{I_i \omega_i}{I_f} \\ \omega_f &= \frac{4.0 \times 2.0}{1.5} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation shows that as the moment of inertia decreases, the angular velocity must increase to keep the total momentum constant.

steps.title: Calculate final result

steps.title: Calculate initial kinetic energy
steps.content:
To find the initial rotational kinetic energy ($E_{k,i}$), we use the formula for rotational kinetic energy from **Topic A.4 (Rigid body mechanics)**:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

Substituting the given initial values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,i} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times (2.0)^2 \\ E_{k,i} &= 8.0 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This earns the first mark.

steps.title: Determine final angular velocity
steps.content:
Since no external torques act on the skater, angular momentum ($L$) is conserved ($L_i = L_f$). We use the definition $L = I\omega$ to find the final angular velocity ($\omega_f$):

$$\begin{aligned} I_i \omega_i &= I_f \omega_f \\ 4.0 \times 2.0 &= 1.5 \times \omega_f \\ 8.0 &= 1.5 \times \omega_f \\ \omega_f &= \frac{8.0}{1.5} \approx 5.33 \text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,f} &= \frac{1}{2} I_f \omega_f^2 \\ E_{k,f} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times (5.33...)^2 \\ E_{k,f} &\approx 21.3 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation provides the second mark.

steps.content:
To use the Energy-Momentum approach, we first find the initial angular momentum ($L$). Although not explicitly in the formula list, students are expected to know the definition of angular momentum for a rigid body:

$$L = I \omega$$

Substituting the initial values:
$$L = 4.0 \times 2.0 = 8.0\ \text{kg m}^2 \text{s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate initial kinetic energy
steps.content:
We calculate the initial rotational kinetic energy ($E_{k,i}$) using the standard formula from the Mechanics section:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,i} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times (2.0)^2 \\ E_{k,i} &= 8.0\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculating initial rotational kinetic energy

steps.title: Apply energy-momentum relationship
steps.content:
Since no external torques act on the skater, angular momentum ($L$) is conserved ($L_i = L_f$). We can express kinetic energy in terms of momentum and inertia using the relationship:

$$E_k = \frac{L^2}{2I}$$

This formula is useful because $L$ remains constant while $I$ changes. We apply this to find the final kinetic energy ($E_{k,f}$):

steps.title: Calculate final kinetic energy
steps.content:
Using the conserved value of $L = 8.0$ and the final moment of inertia $I_f = 1.5$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,f} &= \frac{8.0^2}{2 \times 1.5} \\ E_{k,f} &= \frac{64}{3.0} \\ E_{k,f} &\approx 21.3\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

Final Answers:
*   Initial $E_{k,i} = 8.0\ \text{J}$
*   Final $E_{k,f} = 21.3\ \text{J}$

steps.title: Identify initial rotation parameters
steps.content:
First, let's identify the starting values given in the problem for the figure skater's rotation:

*   Initial moment of inertia ($I_i$) = $4.0\ \text{kg m}^2$
*   Initial angular speed ($\omega_i$) = $2.0\ \text{rad s}^{-1}$
*   Final moment of inertia ($I_f$) = $1.5\ \text{kg m}^2$

These concepts are part of **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

steps.title: Calculate initial kinetic energy
steps.content:
Using the rotational equivalent of the kinetic energy formula, $$E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$, we can find the initial energy ($E_{k,i}$):

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,i} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times (2.0)^2 \\ E_{k,i} &= 2.0 \times 4.0 \\ E_{k,i} &= 8.0\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

This earns the first mark.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate initial rotational kinetic energy.

steps.title: Establish the proportional relationship
steps.content:
Since no external torques act on the skater, angular momentum ($L = I\omega$) is conserved. We can rewrite kinetic energy in terms of $L$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \frac{(I\omega)^2}{2I} = \frac{L^2}{2I}$$

Because $L$ is constant, we can see that rotational kinetic energy is inversely proportional to the moment of inertia:

$$E_k \propto \frac{1}{I}$$

This gives us the ratio:

$$\frac{E_{k,f}}{E_{k,i}} = \frac{I_i}{I_f}$$

steps.title: Determine final kinetic energy
steps.content:
Now we apply the proportional reasoning to find the final kinetic energy ($E_{k,f}$) by multiplying the initial energy by the ratio of the moments of inertia:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{k,f} &= E_{k,i} \times \frac{I_i}{I_f} \\ E_{k,f} &= 8.0 \times \frac{4.0}{1.5} \\ E_{k,f} &\approx 21.3\ \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the final result in the markscheme.

parts.content: Explain the physical origin of the change in kinetic energy.
parts.markscheme:
Work is done by the skater pulling in her arms against centrifugal effects (or towards the axis of rotation) <marks>M1</marks>

Internal work done by the skater's muscles is converted into the increase in rotational kinetic energy <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

steps.title: Identify work done
steps.content: To pull her arms inward toward the axis of rotation, the skater must apply an inward force. This force acts over the distance her arms move. In physics, when a force is applied over a displacement, work is done. Specifically, the skater is doing work against the "centrifugal effects" (the inertia of her arms wanting to continue in their current circular path).

steps.title: Link work to energy
steps.content: This work is not performed by an external force, but rather by the skater's own muscles. This "internal work" is fueled by the conversion of **chemical energy** stored in her muscles. According to the work-energy principle, the work done on the system results in the observed increase in **rotational kinetic energy**.

steps.title: Summarize the origin
steps.content: In summary, the physical origin of the extra kinetic energy is the **chemical energy** from the skater's body. By doing work to pull her mass closer to the center, she effectively "pumps" energy into her own rotation, causing her to spin faster and increasing her kinetic energy.

parts.content: Discuss how this example illustrates conservation laws in rotational motion.
parts.markscheme:
Angular momentum is conserved because the net external torque acting on the system is zero <marks>R1</marks>

Rotational kinetic energy is not conserved because internal work is performed by the skater, increasing the rotational speed <marks>R1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

steps.title: Analyze Angular Momentum Conservation
steps.content:
In rotational motion, angular momentum ($L$) is conserved if there is no net external torque acting on the system. 

In this scenario, we assume the ice provides negligible friction. Because the force used to pull her arms in is an **internal force**, it does not create an external torque. Therefore, the total angular momentum of the skater remains constant throughout the move.

$$\text{Angular momentum is conserved because } \tau_{\text{net}} = 0$$

highlights.text: Angular momentum is conserved because the net external torque acting on the system is zero

steps.title: Analyze Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
While angular momentum is conserved, **rotational kinetic energy is not**. 

As the skater pulls her arms inward, she must apply a force over a distance to overcome the "centrifugal effect" (the tendency of her arms to stay out). This means she is performing **internal work**. 

According to the work-energy theorem, this work is converted into rotational kinetic energy, which is why her angular speed increases so significantly. Since energy is being added to the rotation by her muscles, the kinetic energy of the system increases.

highlights.text: Rotational kinetic energy is not conserved because internal work is performed by the skater

steps.title: Summary of Conservation Laws
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks for this discussion, you must mention both laws:

1.  **Angular Momentum:** Conserved because the net external torque is zero.
2.  **Kinetic Energy:** Not conserved because the skater does internal work to pull her arms in, increasing the energy of the system.

This is a classic **Topic A.4 (Rigid Body Mechanics)** concept. Understanding the difference between internal work and external torque is key to mastering rotational dynamics!

steps.title: Calculate Angular Momentum
steps.content:
First, we calculate the initial angular momentum ($L$) using the formula $L = I\omega$. Since there is no net external torque acting on the skater, angular momentum must be conserved ($L_1 = L_2$).

Using the values provided:
- Initial moment of inertia $I_1 = 4.0\text{ kg m}^2$
- Initial angular speed $\omega_1 = 2.0\text{ rad s}^{-1}$

$$L = 4.0 \times 2.0 = 8.0\text{ kg m}^2 \text{s}^{-1}$$

highlights.text: moment of inertia of 4.0 kg m^2

steps.title: Find Final Angular Velocity
steps.content:
Because angular momentum is conserved ($I_1\omega_1 = I_2\omega_2$), we can find the new angular speed ($\omega_2$) after she pulls her arms in and the moment of inertia decreases to $1.5\text{ kg m}^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} 8.0 &= 1.5 \times \omega_2 \\ \omega_2 &= \frac{8.0}{1.5} \approx 5.33\text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate final angular velocity

steps.title: Compare Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
Now we compare the initial and final rotational kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$). 

**Initial Kinetic Energy:**
$$E_{k1} = \frac{1}{2}(4.0)(2.0)^2 = 8.0\text{ J}$$

**Final Kinetic Energy:**
$$E_{k2} = \frac{1}{2}(1.5)(5.33)^2 \approx 21.3\text{ J}$$

Notice that the kinetic energy has increased significantly, meaning energy is **not** conserved.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.5 * 4 * 2^2 for initial and 0.5 * 1.5 * (5.3333)^2 for final.

calculator.description: Verify initial and final kinetic energies

steps.title: Final Discussion
steps.content:
Based on our calculations, we can conclude:

1.  **Angular Momentum is conserved:** As calculated, $L$ remains constant at $8.0\text{ kg m}^2 \text{s}^{-1}$ because there is no net external torque.
2.  **Rotational Kinetic Energy is NOT conserved:** It increased from $8.0\text{ J}$ to $21.3\text{ J}$. This occurs because the skater performs **internal work** by using her muscles to pull her arms inward against the centrifugal effect, which adds energy to the system.

steps.title: Relate to linear motion
steps.content:
To solve this using **Direct Recall**, we look for the rotational equivalent of Newton’s Second Law ($F = ma$). In rotational dynamics, each linear quantity has a corresponding angular partner:

*   Force ($F$) corresponds to torque ($\tau$)
*   Mass ($m$) corresponds to moment of inertia ($I$)
*   Linear acceleration ($a$) corresponds to angular acceleration ($\alpha$)

This concept is a core part of **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

highlights.text: State the equation that relates torque to angular acceleration for a rotating body.

steps.title: State the rotational equation
steps.content:
By substituting the rotational analogs into Newton's Second Law, we find the fundamental equation for rotational dynamics:

$$\tau = I\alpha$$

Where:
*   $\tau$ is the resultant torque (in $\text{N m}$)
*   $I$ is the moment of inertia (in $\text{kg m}^2$)
*   $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration (in $\text{rad s}^{-2}$)

 - Correct unit <marks> 1 mark </marks> and method <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
To calculate the moment of inertia, we first need to identify the mass and the radius of the disc from the question.

*   Mass ($M$) = $2.0\ \text{kg}$
*   Radius ($R$) = $0.30\ \text{m}$

steps.title: Substitute into the formula
steps.content:
The question provides the specific formula for the moment of inertia ($I$) of a disc. We simply substitute our known values into this equation:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times (0.30)^2$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, we perform the calculation. Notice that $0.5 \times 2.0$ equals $1.0$, which simplifies our work. Squaring the radius $0.30$ gives $0.090$.

$$I = 1.0 \times 0.090 = 0.090\ \text{kg m}^2$$

Don't forget the units! Since moment of inertia is mass times radius squared, the units are $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$. This question is worth 2 marks: one for the correct calculation method and one for the final value with units.

steps.title: Calculate Moment of Inertia
steps.content:
In rotational dynamics, the moment of inertia ($I$) represents an object's resistance to rotational acceleration. For a uniform disc rotating about its central axis, we use the formula from **Topic A.4 (Rigid body mechanics)**:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$$

Substituting the given values:

$$I = 0.5 \times 2.0\ \text{kg} \times (0.30\ \text{m})^2$$
$$I = 0.090\ \text{kg m}^2$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moment of inertia of the disc.

steps.title: Calculate Angular Acceleration
steps.content:
Next, we apply the rotational equivalent of Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$). In rotational motion, this is expressed as:

$$\tau = I\alpha$$

Where:
*   $\tau$ is the torque
*   $I$ is the moment of inertia
*   $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration

Rearranging for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{I}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{0.45}{0.090}$$

steps.title: State Final Answer
steps.content:
Using the calculation from the previous step, we find the final value for the angular acceleration. 

$$\alpha = 5.0\ \text{rad s}^{-2}$$

$60=\frac{1}{2}I(8^2)=32I \Rightarrow I=\frac{60}{32}=1.875\ \text{kg m}^2$.

This is the ratio of rolling speed to sliding speed ($v_{roll} = \sqrt{5/7} v_{slide}$), answering the opposite of what was asked.

For a rolling solid sphere, the total kinetic energy is $K =  rac{1}{2}mv^2 +  rac{1}{2}I\omega^2 =  rac{1}{2}mv^2 +  rac{1}{2}( rac{2}{5}mr^2)( rac{v}{r})^2 =  rac{7}{10}mv^2$.

On a frictionless incline, the sphere slides without rotating, so $mgh =  rac{1}{2}m(v')^2$. 

Equating the two expressions for $mgh$: 
$ rac{1}{2}m(v')^2 =  rac{7}{10}mv^2$ 
$(v')^2 =  rac{14}{10}v^2 =  rac{7}{5}v^2$ 
$v' = \sqrt{ rac{7}{5}}v$.
This assumes the speeds are identical, neglecting the fact that in the rolling case, some potential energy is converted into rotational kinetic energy.

parts.content: Determine the angular acceleration $\alpha$ of the disk due to the applied force. The moment of inertia $I$ for a solid disk about its center is given by $I=\frac{1}{2}MR^2$.

steps.title: Identify the relevant concepts
steps.content:
To find the angular acceleration, we need to connect the linear force applied to the rotation of the disk. This involves two main concepts from **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**:
1. **Torque ($	au$)**: The rotational equivalent of force.
2. **Moment of Inertia ($I$)**: The rotational equivalent of mass.

We will use the rotational version of Newton's Second Law: 
$$\tau = I\alpha$$

steps.title: Calculate the applied torque
steps.content:
Torque is defined as the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation. Since the force is applied tangentially, the distance is simply the radius $R$.

$$\tau = F \times R$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\tau = 10 \, \text{N} \times 0.40 \, \text{m} = 4.0 \, \text{N m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the torque.

highlights.text: force F=10 N is applied tangentially at the rim

steps.title: Calculate the moment of inertia
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the moment of inertia $I$ using the formula provided in the question for a solid disk:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$

Substituting the mass and radius:
$$I = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \, \text{kg} \times (0.40 \, \text{m})^2$$
$$I = 0.16 \, \text{kg m}^2$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moment of inertia.

steps.title: Determine the angular acceleration
steps.content:
Finally, we rearrange the rotational Newton's Second Law formula to solve for angular acceleration $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{I}$$

Using our calculated values:
$$\alpha = \frac{4.0 \, \text{N m}}{0.16 \, \text{kg m}^2} = 25 \, \text{rad s}^{-2}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final angular acceleration.

steps.title: Identify required physics laws
steps.content:
To find the angular acceleration, we look at **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics (HL only)**. We need the rotational equivalent of Newton's second law, which relates torque ($\tau$), moment of inertia ($I$), and angular acceleration ($\alpha$):

$$\tau = I\alpha$$

steps.title: Express torque and inertia
steps.content:
The torque produced by a tangential force $F$ at a distance $R$ from the axle is given by:
$$\tau = R \cdot F$$

We are also given the formula for the moment of inertia for a solid disk:
$$\begin{gathered} I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2 \end{gathered}$$

steps.title: Combine and simplify algebraically
steps.content:
Substitute the expressions for $\tau$ and $I$ into the equation $\tau = I\alpha$:

$$R \cdot F = \left( \frac{1}{2}MR^2 \right) \alpha$$

Now, solve for $\alpha$ and simplify the radius $R$:

$$\begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{R \cdot F}{\frac{1}{2}MR^2} \\ \alpha &= \frac{2F}{MR} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Substitute the given numerical values into our simplified expression:
- $F = 10 \, \mathrm{N}$
- $M = 2.0 \, \mathrm{kg}$
- $R = 0.40 \, \mathrm{m}$

$$\begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{2 \times 10}{2.0 \times 0.40} \\ \alpha &= \frac{20}{0.80} \\ \alpha &= 25 \, \mathrm{rad\,s^{-2}} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, 1 mark is for the relationship $\tau=I \alpha$ and 1 mark for the correct calculation.

parts.content: Assuming the disk starts from rest, calculate the angular velocity $\omega$ of the disk after $5.0$ seconds of applying the force.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the kinematic equation $\omega=\omega_0+\alpha t$, where $\omega_0=0 \, \mathrm{rad\,s^{-1}}, \alpha=25 \, \mathrm{rad\,s^{-2}}$, and $t=5.0 \, \mathrm{s}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\omega=0+25 \times 5=125 \, \mathrm{rad\,s^{-1}}
$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To solve this using rotational kinematics, we first identify our known values from the problem. This topic is part of **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

*   Radius $R = 0.40\, \text{m}$
*   Mass $M = 2.0\, \text{kg}$
*   Applied tangential force $F = 10\, \text{N}$
*   Time $t = 5.0\, \text{s}$
*   Initial angular velocity $\omega_0 = 0\, \text{rad s}^{-1}$ (since it starts from rest)

steps.title: Calculate the torque
steps.content:
Torque ($\tau$) is the rotational analog of force. Because the force is applied tangentially at the rim, the lever arm is the radius $R$ and the angle is $90^\circ$.

$$\tau = F \times R$$
$$\tau = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0\, \text{N m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the torque produced by the tangential force.

steps.title: Calculate moment of inertia
steps.content:
For a uniform solid disk rotating about its center, the moment of inertia ($I$) is calculated as:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$
$$I = 0.5 \times 2.0 \times (0.40)^2 = 0.16\, \text{kg m}^2$$

calculator.description: Calculate the moment of inertia for the solid disk.

steps.title: Find angular acceleration
steps.content:
Using the rotational version of Newton's second law, $\tau = I\alpha$, we solve for the angular acceleration ($\alpha$):

$$\begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{\tau}{I} \\ \alpha &= \frac{4.0}{0.16} \\ \alpha &= 25\, \text{rad s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide torque by moment of inertia to find angular acceleration.

steps.title: Calculate final angular velocity
steps.content:
Now we apply the rotational kinematic equation. This is the rotational equivalent of the linear motion equation $$v = u + at$$ found in your data booklet.

$$\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$$

Substituting our values (where $\omega_0 = 0$ and $t = 5.0$):

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= 0 + (25 \times 5.0) \\ \omega &= 125\, \text{rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Multiply angular acceleration by time to find the final angular velocity.

steps.title: Calculate the applied torque
steps.content:
First, we need to find the torque ($\tau$) acting on the disk. Torque is defined as the force multiplied by the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation. 

Since the force is applied tangentially at the rim, the distance is the radius $R$ and the angle is $90^\circ$:

$$\tau = F \times R$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\tau = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0 \text{ N m}$$

steps.title: Calculate moment of inertia
steps.content:
For a uniform solid disk rotating about its center, the moment of inertia ($I$) is given by the formula:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$

Using the mass and radius provided:
$$\begin{aligned} I &= \frac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times (0.40)^2 \\ I &= 1.0 \times 0.16 \\ I &= 0.16 \text{ kg m}^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the angular impulse
steps.content:
Angular impulse is the product of torque and the time interval over which it acts. This is analogous to linear impulse ($J = F \Delta t$).

$$\text{Angular Impulse} = \tau \times \Delta t$$

Using our calculated torque and the time given:
$$\text{Angular Impulse} = 4.0 \times 5.0 = 20.0 \text{ N m s}$$

steps.title: Apply Angular Impulse-Momentum theorem
steps.content:
The Angular Impulse-Momentum theorem states that the change in angular momentum ($\Delta L$) is equal to the angular impulse:

$$\Delta L = \text{Angular Impulse}$$
$$I\omega_f - I\omega_i = \tau \Delta t$$

Since the disk **starts from rest**, the initial angular velocity $\omega_i = 0$. Therefore:

$$I\omega_f = \tau \Delta t$$

steps.title: Solve for angular velocity
steps.content:
Finally, we solve for the final angular velocity ($\omega_f$) by dividing the angular impulse by the moment of inertia:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega_f &= \frac{\tau \Delta t}{I} \\ \omega_f &= \frac{20.0}{0.16} \end{aligned}$$

$$\omega_f = 125 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

parts.content: Determine the rotational kinetic energy $E_k$ of the disk after $5.0$ seconds.
parts.markscheme:
- The rotational kinetic energy is given by $E_k=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
E_k=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.16 \times(125)^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.16 \times 15625=1250 \, \mathrm{J}
$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Calculate the moment of inertia
steps.content:
To find the rotational kinetic energy, we first need the moment of inertia $I$ of the disk. For a uniform solid disk of mass $M$ and radius $R$, the moment of inertia is given by:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\begin{aligned} I &= \frac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times 0.40^2 \\ I &= 0.16 \, \mathrm{kg \, m^2} \end{aligned}$$

This concept is part of **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

steps.title: Find the angular acceleration
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the torque $\tau$ applied to the disk and the resulting angular acceleration $\alpha$. Since the force is applied tangentially at the rim:

$$\tau = F \times R$$
$$\tau = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0 \, \mathrm{N \, m}$$

Using the rotational analogue of Newton's second law ($ \tau = I\alpha $):

$$\begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{\tau}{I} \\ \alpha &= \frac{4.0}{0.16} = 25 \, \mathrm{rad \, s^{-2}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine final angular velocity
steps.content:
Now we use the rotational kinematics equation to find the angular velocity $\omega$ after the given time $t$. The disk starts from rest, so the initial angular velocity $\omega_0 = 0$.

$$\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$$
$$\omega = 0 + (25 \times 5.0)$$
$$\omega = 125 \, \mathrm{rad \, s^{-1}}$$

steps.title: Calculate rotational kinetic energy
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the rotational kinetic energy $E_k$ using the formula provided in the markscheme:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{1}{2} \times 0.16 \times (125)^2 \\ E_k &= 0.08 \times 15625 \\ E_k &= 1250 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final rotational kinetic energy.

steps.title: Calculate Torque and Inertia
steps.content:
First, we need to determine the torque ($\tau$) and the moment of inertia ($I$) for the disk. Torque is calculated by multiplying the force by the perpendicular distance from the pivot (the radius).

$$\tau = F \times R$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\tau = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0 \text{ N m}$$

Next, we find the moment of inertia for a solid disk using the formula $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$ (from **Topic A.4**):

$$I = \frac{1}{2} \times 2.0 \times 0.40^2 = 0.16 \text{ kg m}^2$$

steps.title: Find Angular Acceleration
steps.content:
Using Newton's Second Law for rotation, we can find the angular acceleration ($\alpha$). The relationship is $\tau = I\alpha$, which is the rotational equivalent of $F=ma$.

$$\begin{aligned} \alpha &= \frac{\tau}{I} \\ \alpha &= \frac{4.0}{0.16} \\ \alpha &= 25 \text{ rad s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the angular acceleration by dividing torque by the moment of inertia.

steps.title: Determine Angular Displacement
steps.content:
Since the disk starts from rest and is subjected to a constant torque, we can use the rotational version of the kinematic equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$. For rotation, this becomes $\theta = \omega_i t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$.

Given the disk starts from rest ($\omega_i = 0$) and we are looking for the state after $5.0$ seconds:

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (5.0)^2 \\ \theta &= 0.5 \times 25 \times 25 \\ \theta &= 312.5 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the angular displacement (theta) using the kinematic formula.

steps.title: Calculate Rotational Work
steps.content:
The work-energy theorem states that the work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy ($W = \Delta E_k$). For rotation, the work done is $W = \tau \theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= W = \tau \theta \\ E_k &= 4.0 \times 312.5 \\ E_k &= 1250 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This method confirms that after 5 seconds, the disk has gained $1250 \text{ J}$ of rotational kinetic energy.

calculator.description: Calculate the final work done by multiplying torque and displacement.

steps.title: Calculate the applied torque
steps.content:
To use the angular impulse approach, we first need the torque ($\tau$) applied to the disk. Torque is defined as the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation.

Since the force $F = 10 \, \text{N}$ is applied tangentially at the rim ($R = 0.40 \, \text{m}$), the torque is:

$$\tau = F \times R$$
$$\tau = 10 \times 0.40 = 4.0 \, \text{N m}$$

steps.title: Find the angular momentum
steps.content:
Next, we find the change in angular momentum ($L$). Just as linear impulse is $F \Delta t$, angular impulse is defined as the change in angular momentum:

$$\Delta L = \tau \Delta t$$

Given the disk starts from rest and the force is applied for $5.0 \, \text{s}$:

$$L_{\text{final}} = 4.0 \times 5.0 = 20.0 \, \text{kg m}^2 \text{s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate moment of inertia
steps.content:
For a uniform solid disk, the moment of inertia ($I$) is calculated using the standard formula from **Topic A.4 (Rigid Body Mechanics)**:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} M R^2$$

Substituting the values $M = 2.0 \, \text{kg}$ and $R = 0.40 \, \text{m}$:

$$I = 0.5 \times 2.0 \times (0.40)^2 = 0.16 \, \text{kg m}^2$$

steps.title: Determine rotational kinetic energy
steps.content:
Finally, we can relate rotational kinetic energy ($E_k$) to angular momentum ($L$) and moment of inertia ($I$). This is the rotational equivalent of the linear formula $E_k = \frac{p^2}{2m}$:

$$E_k = \frac{L^2}{2I}$$

Substituting our calculated values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{20^2}{2 \times 0.16} \\ E_k &= \frac{400}{0.32} \\ E_k &= 1250 \, \text{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final rotational kinetic energy.

steps.title: Calculate tangential acceleration
steps.content:
First, we find the tangential acceleration ($a$) of the rim. For a solid disk with a force applied at the edge, the relationship between force and tangential acceleration is derived from torque and the moment of inertia ($I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$):

$$a = \frac{2F}{M}$$

Substituting the values from the question:

$$a = \frac{2 \times 10}{2.0} = 10 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$$

steps.title: Find the linear distance
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the linear distance ($s$) that a point on the rim travels in $5.0$ seconds. Using the kinematic equation for an object starting from rest ($u=0$):

$$\begin{aligned} s &= ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2 \\ s &= 0 + \frac{1}{2}(10)(5.0)^2 \\ s &= 125 \, \mathrm{m} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation is based on equations found in **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance s using the values a=10 and t=5.

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
The work done by the tangential force is the product of the force and the distance moved along the path. Since the force is always applied tangentially (parallel to the motion of the rim), we use:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= Fs \\ W &= 10 \times 125 \\ W &= 1250 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

This follows the definition of work in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

calculator.description: Calculate the work done W = 10 * 125.

steps.title: State final kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the Work-Energy Theorem, the work done on the disk is equal to its change in rotational kinetic energy ($W = \Delta E_k$). Since the disk started from rest, the final rotational kinetic energy is:

$$E_k = 1250 \, \mathrm{J}$$

This is a efficient way to solve rotational energy problems without having to find the angular velocity first.

parts.content: The disk is now brought to rest by applying a tangential force at the rim in the opposite direction. If the disk is brought to rest in $t=2.0 \, \mathrm{s}$, calculate the average angular deceleration $\alpha_{\text{avg}}$ and the magnitude of the tangential force required to stop the disk.

$$
\alpha_{\text{avg}}=\frac{\Delta \omega}{t}=\frac{125-0}{2.0}=62.5 \, \mathrm{rad\,s^{-2}}
$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

- The torque needed to stop the disk is:

$$
\tau=I \alpha_{\text{avg}}=0.16 \times 62.5=10.0 \, \mathrm{N\,m}
$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

- The force required to stop the disk is:

$$
F=\frac{\tau}{R}=\frac{10.0}{0.40}=25 \, \mathrm{N}
$$

External torque is zero, so angular momentum ($L$) is conserved.
The child moves to the centre, decreasing the moment of inertia ($I$) of the system.
Since $L = I\omega$, if $I$ decreases, angular velocity ($\omega$) increases.
Rotational kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{L^2}{2I}$. Since $L$ is constant and $I$ decreases, $E_k$ increases. (Work is done by the child walking inwards).

questions.specification:
A student investigates the motion of small objects placed at different distances from the center of a rotating turntable. The turntable rotates at a constant angular speed $\omega$, and the student increases the speed gradually until the object begins to slip outward. The critical angular velocity $\omega_{c}$ at which slipping occurs is recorded for different radial positions. The results are shown in the table below.

| Radius $r(\mathrm{~m})$ | Critical angular velocity $\omega_{c}(\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s})$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.05 | 14.0 |
| 0.08 | 11.1 |
| 0.10 | 9.9 |
| 0.12 | 9.1 |
| 0.15 | 8.2 |

parts.content: Describe the physical condition that causes the object to begin slipping on the turntable.
parts.markscheme:
- The only force providing centripetal acceleration is static friction. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Slipping occurs when required centripetal force exceeds the maximum static friction force. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the centripetal force
steps.content:
For an object to move in a circle on a flat turntable, there must be a net force acting toward the center. This is called the **centripetal force**. In this scenario, the only horizontal force acting on the object is the **static friction** between the object and the turntable surface.

This concept is a core part of **Topic A.2.4 Circular motion**.

steps.title: Define required centripetal force
steps.content:
According to Newton's Second Law, the net force required to keep an object of mass $m$ moving in a circle is given by $F = ma$. Using the formula for centripetal acceleration from the data booklet, the **required centripetal force** is:

$$F_{\text{required}} = m\omega^2r$$

As the angular velocity $\omega$ increases, the amount of force required to keep the object from moving in a straight line also increases.

steps.title: Identify the friction limit
steps.content:
Static friction is a "responsive" force; it provides exactly as much force as needed to prevent slipping, up to a certain **maximum limit** ($f_{s,max}$). 

This limit depends on the nature of the surfaces and the normal force, but for a constant mass and surface, $f_{s,max}$ is a fixed value.

steps.title: Determine the slipping condition
steps.content:
Slipping occurs when the **required centripetal force** ($m\omega^2r$) becomes greater than the **maximum static friction force** that the surfaces can provide. 

At this critical point $\omega_c$, the friction can no longer provide the necessary acceleration to keep the object in its circular path, and it begins to slip outward.

**Marking Note:** To earn full marks, you must explicitly state that friction provides the centripetal force and that slipping happens when the required force exceeds the maximum friction available.

steps.title: Identify the centripetal force
steps.content: To move in a circle, an object must experience a centripetal acceleration directed toward the center of the turntable. In this scenario, the only horizontal force acting on the object is the static friction between the object and the turntable surface. Therefore, static friction is the force providing the necessary centripetal acceleration to keep the object moving in a circle.

steps.title: Determine the required acceleration
steps.content:
According to the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet, the centripetal acceleration $a$ required for an object at a distance $r$ from the center is:

$$a = \omega^2 r$$

As the student increases the angular speed, the required acceleration for the object to maintain its circular path also increases.

steps.title: Define the slipping condition
steps.content:
Static friction has a maximum limit. This means there is a maximum acceleration that friction can provide to the object. Slipping begins when the required centripetal acceleration exceeds the maximum acceleration that the friction between the surfaces can sustain.

From Newton's second law, $$F = ma$$. If the required acceleration $\omega^2 r$ becomes greater than the maximum acceleration possible from static friction, the object can no longer follow the circular path and begins to slip outward.

highlights.text: critical angular velocity \omega_{c} at which slipping occurs

parts.content: Show that the critical angular velocity is given by the expression $\omega_{c}^{2}=\frac{\mu g}{r}$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of static friction.
parts.markscheme:
- Set centripetal force equal to maximum static friction: $m \omega_{c}^{2} r=\mu m g$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rearrange to get: $\omega_{c}^{2}=\frac{\mu g}{r}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the centripetal force
steps.content:
To keep an object moving in a circle, a centripetal force is required. According to **Topic A.2: Circular motion**, the acceleration for an object rotating at a constant angular speed $\omega$ is given by $a = \omega^2 r$. 

Using Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$), we can express the required centripetal force $F_c$ as:

$$F_c = m \omega^2 r$$

steps.title: Define the frictional force
steps.content:
The force providing this centripetal acceleration is the friction between the object and the turntable. For a horizontal surface, the normal force is equal to the weight of the object ($mg$). 

The maximum static frictional force $F_f$ that can act on the object is defined by the coefficient of friction $\mu$:

$$F_f = \mu mg$$

steps.title: Equate the forces
steps.content:
At the moment the object is about to slip, the required centripetal force is exactly equal to the maximum available static friction. We use the critical angular velocity $\omega_c$ for this specific condition.

$$\begin{aligned} F_c &= F_f \\ m \omega_c^2 r &= \mu mg \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for the final expression
steps.content:
Now, we simplify the equation to reach the required form. Notice that the mass $m$ appears on both sides, so it cancels out:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega_c^2 r &= \mu g \\ \omega_c^2 &= \frac{\mu g}{r} \end{aligned}$$

This shows that the critical angular velocity depends only on the friction, gravity, and the radius, not the mass of the object.

steps.title: Identify the equilibrium condition
steps.content: For the object to stay on the rotating turntable without slipping, the friction force must provide the necessary centripetal force. At the exact moment it begins to slip, the centripetal force is equal to the maximum static friction available.

steps.title: Equate linear forces
steps.content:
We start by setting the linear expression for centripetal force equal to the maximum static friction. 

The centripetal force is given by:
$$F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

The maximum static friction is:
$$f_{max} = \mu R = \mu mg$$

Equating these gives us our starting point:
$$\frac{mv^2}{r} = \mu mg$$

steps.title: Substitute angular velocity
steps.content:
To transition from linear speed $v$ to angular speed $\omega$, we use the standard relationship $v = \omega r$. Substituting this into our force equation:

$$\frac{m(\omega r)^2}{r} = \mu mg$$

Now, expand the squared term:
$$\frac{m \omega^2 r^2}{r} = \mu mg$$

steps.title: Simplify and solve
steps.content:
Finally, we simplify the equation by canceling the mass $m$ and one factor of the radius $r$ from the left side:

$$\begin{aligned} m \omega_c^2 r &= \mu mg \\ \omega_c^2 r &= \mu g \\ \omega_c^2 &= \frac{\mu g}{r} \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the expression for the critical angular velocity where slipping occurs.

steps.title: Identify the required acceleration
steps.content:
To keep an object moving in a circle of radius $r$ at a constant angular velocity $\omega$, it must undergo centripetal acceleration directed towards the center. This is a key concept in **Topic A.2.4 Circular motion**. From the IB data booklet, the formula for this acceleration is:

$$a = \omega^{2}r$$

steps.title: Determine maximum possible acceleration
steps.content:
The acceleration is provided by the static friction between the object and the turntable. Applying Newton's Second Law, $F = ma$, we know that the maximum acceleration $a_{max}$ occurs when the friction force is at its maximum limit, $F_{f} = \mu mg$. 

$$\begin{aligned} m a_{max} &= \mu m g \\ a_{max} &= \mu g \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Equate at the slipping point
steps.content:
The object begins to slip at the **critical angular velocity** $\omega_{c}$ when the required centripetal acceleration exactly matches the maximum acceleration that friction can provide. We set the two expressions for acceleration equal to each other:

$$\omega_{c}^{2}r = \mu g$$

steps.title: Rearrange for the final expression
steps.content:
To find the final expression, we simply rearrange the equation to solve for $\omega_{c}^{2}$ by dividing both sides by $r$:

$$\omega_{c}^{2} = \frac{\mu g}{r}$$

This shows the relationship between the critical angular velocity, the coefficient of friction, and the radius.

parts.content: Plot a graph of $\omega_{c}^{2}$ against $\frac{1}{r}$ using the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct axes: $\omega_{c}^{2}$ on vertical axis, $\frac{1}{r}$ on horizontal axis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Points plotted accurately with best-fit straight line. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Transform the independent variable
steps.content:
To plot the requested graph, we first need to calculate the reciprocal of the radial distance, $$\frac{1}{r}$$, for each data point in the table. This will be our horizontal axis variable.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{0.05} &= 20.0 \text{ m}^{-1} \\ \frac{1}{0.08} &= 12.5 \text{ m}^{-1} \\ \frac{1}{0.10} &= 10.0 \text{ m}^{-1} \\ \frac{1}{0.12} &\approx 8.3 \text{ m}^{-1} \\ \frac{1}{0.15} &\approx 6.7 \text{ m}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Transform the dependent variable
steps.content:
Next, we square the critical angular velocity values, $$\omega_c^2$$, to find the vertical axis coordinates:

$$\begin{aligned} 14.0^2 &= 196 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2} \\ 11.1^2 &= 123 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2} \\ 9.9^2 &= 98 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2} \\ 9.1^2 &= 83 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2} \\ 8.2^2 &= 67 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the axes
steps.content: According to the markscheme, the vertical axis must represent $$\omega_c^2$$ and the horizontal axis must represent $$\frac{1}{r}$$. Ensure you label both axes with their respective units: $$\text{rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2}$$ for the vertical axis and $$\text{m}^{-1}$$ for the horizontal axis.

highlights.text: Correct axes: $\omega_{c}^{2}$ on vertical axis, $\frac{1}{r}$ on horizontal axis.

steps.title: Plot and draw best-fit
steps.content:
Plot the points calculated in the previous steps. Once plotted, use a ruler to draw a single straight line of best fit that passes as close as possible to all points. In circular motion experiments involving friction, this relationship is expected to be linear because:

$$a = \omega^2 r \implies \omega^2 = a \left( \frac{1}{r} \right)$$

where the centripetal acceleration $$a$$ is constant at the point of slipping.

parts.content: What does the shape of your graph suggest about the relationship between $\omega_{c}$ and $r$ ?
parts.markscheme: - Straight line through origin confirms $\omega_{c}^{2} \propto \frac{1}{r}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define the linearized variables
steps.content:
To analyze the relationship between the critical angular velocity $\omega_{c}$ and the radius $r$, we look at a linearized version of the data. Typically, in circular motion experiments involving friction and centripetal acceleration, we plot $\omega_{c}^{2}$ against $\frac{1}{r}$. 

This follows from the physics of circular motion (Topic A.2.4), where the centripetal acceleration is given by:

$$a = \omega^{2}r$$

steps.title: Verify the linear trend
steps.content: Let's check if the data supports a linear relationship between $\omega_{c}^{2}$ and $\frac{1}{r}$. If we calculate $\omega_{c}^{2}$ for each point, we can see if they change proportionally with $\frac{1}{r}$.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the squares of the first and last recorded velocities.

calculator.description: Calculate squared angular velocity for the first and last data points to check proportionality.

steps.title: Interpret the graphical features
steps.content:
In physics, a **straight line passing through the origin** is a clear indicator of **direct proportionality** between the variables on the $y$ and $x$ axes. 

Since the graph of $\omega_{c}^{2}$ versus $\frac{1}{r}$ results in such a line, it confirms that the square of the angular velocity is directly proportional to the reciprocal of the radius.

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
Based on the linearized proportionality analysis, the shape of the graph (a straight line through the origin) suggests that:

$$\omega_{c}^{2} \propto \frac{1}{r}$$

steps.title: Observe the general trend
steps.content: By looking at the values in the data table, we can see that as the radius $r$ increases, the critical angular velocity $\omega_{c}$ decreases. This tells us there is an **inverse** relationship between the two variables, rather than a direct one.

steps.title: Analyze the curve shape
steps.content:
If we were to plot these points, we would see a curve that slopes downward. Specifically, notice the change in $\omega_{c}$ for similar changes in $r$. 

From $r = 0.05$ to $0.08$ (a change of $0.03$ m), $\omega_{c}$ drops by $2.9$ rad/s. 
From $r = 0.12$ to $0.15$ (also a change of $0.03$ m), $\omega_{c}$ only drops by $0.9$ rad/s. 

Since the rate of decrease is getting smaller as $r$ increases, the graph is a non-linear curve that is concave up (it flattens out).

gdc.instructions: Enter the $r$ values in List 1 and $\omega_c$ values in List 2. Use the Scatter Plot function to observe the curved shape that flattens as $r$ increases.

4. Press [2nd] [STAT PLOT] and turn Plot1 ON. 
5. Press [ZOOM] [9] to see the non-linear curve.

calculator.description: Visualize the inverse relationship by plotting the points.

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
The shape of the graph suggests an **inverse-type relationship**. 

Because the critical angular velocity decreases at a decreasing rate as the radius increases, we can conclude that $\omega_{c}$ is inversely proportional to some power of $r$ (mathematically, $\omega_{c} \propto r^{n}$ where $n < 0$). This aligns with the physics of circular motion where centripetal acceleration is given by:

$$a = \omega^{2}r$$

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: In summary, the non-linear, downward-sloping curve indicates that $\omega_{c}$ and $r$ have an **inverse relationship**. Specifically, the data follows a relationship where $\omega_{c}^{2} \propto \frac{1}{r}$, which would produce a straight line if $\omega_{c}^{2}$ were plotted against $\frac{1}{r}$.

steps.title: Analyze the theoretical model
steps.content:
To understand what the graph suggests, we look at the theoretical physics relationship for an object about to slip on a rotating turntable. This occurs when the required centripetal force is provided by the maximum static friction ($f_s = \mu mg$). 

From the provided model comparison, we use the derived equation:
$$\omega_{c}^{2} = \frac{\mu g}{r}$$

This formula is based on concepts from **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**, specifically circular motion where $a = \omega^2 r$.

steps.title: Identify the proportionality
steps.content:
In this experiment, the coefficient of friction ($\mu$) and the acceleration due to gravity ($g$) are constants. Therefore, the relationship can be simplified to show how $\omega_c^2$ depends on the radius $r$:

$$\omega_{c}^{2} \propto \frac{1}{r}$$

This indicates that the square of the critical angular velocity is inversely proportional to the radius.

steps.title: Interpret the graph shape
steps.content:
When a graph of $\omega_c^2$ is plotted against $\frac{1}{r}$, a straight line passing through the origin is expected if the theoretical model is correct. 

Such a trend confirms that the experimental data follows the relationship $\omega_{c}^{2} = \frac{k}{r}$ (where $k = \mu g$), proving that $\omega_c^2$ is indeed proportional to $\frac{1}{r}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate $\omega_c^2 \cdot r$ for the first data point.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the square of the first and last angular velocity values by their respective radii to check for a constant value.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the product $\omega_c^2 \cdot r$ for the first and last points.

calculator.description: We can verify the consistency of the relationship by checking if $\omega_c^2 \cdot r$ is approximately constant for the given data.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
The straight line through the origin suggests that the square of the critical angular velocity is inversely proportional to the radius, confirming the relationship:

$$\omega_{c}^{2} \propto \frac{1}{r}$$

parts.content: Use your graph to estimate the coefficient of static friction $\mu$.
parts.markscheme:
- From the graph of $\omega_{c}^{2}$ vs. $\frac{1}{r}$, the gradient is approximately $9.69\left(\mathrm{rad}^{2} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{m}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using $\omega_{c}^{2}=\frac{\mu g}{r} \Rightarrow$ slope $=\mu g$, so $\mu=\frac{\text { slope }}{g}=\frac{9.69}{9.81} \approx 0.99$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Linearize the physics equation
steps.content:
To relate the graph to the physics, we start with the condition for slipping. The centripetal force is provided by static friction:

$$m \omega_c^2 r = \mu mg$$

Rearranging to match the form of a linear graph ($y = mx$), where $y = \omega_c^2$ and $x = \frac{1}{r}$:

$$\omega_c^2 = (\mu g) \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

This shows that the gradient of the graph is equal to $\mu g$.

steps.title: Calculate linearized data points
steps.content:
To plot the graph or find the gradient, we calculate the values for $\omega_c^2$ and $\frac{1}{r}$ using the table provided.

For example, at the first point:
- $x = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20.0 \text{ m}^{-1}$
- $y = 14.0^2 = 196.0 \text{ rad}^2\text{s}^{-2}$

Doing this for all points allows us to find the gradient of the line of best fit.

gdc.instructions: Enter 1/0.05 and 14^2 on the home screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1/0.05 and 14^2 on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the squared angular velocity and reciprocal radius for the first data point.

steps.title: Identify the gradient
steps.content:
From the line of best fit drawn through the calculated points $(\frac{1}{r}, \omega_c^2)$, the gradient is found to be:

$$\text{gradient} \approx 9.69 \text{ rad}^2\text{s}^{-2}\text{m}$$

This value represents the change in $\omega_c^2$ over the change in $\frac{1}{r}$.

steps.title: Calculate coefficient of friction
steps.content:
Since $\text{gradient} = \mu g$, we rearrange to solve for $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{\text{gradient}}{g}$$

Using $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (or $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ from the data booklet):

$$\mu = \frac{9.69}{9.81} \approx 0.99$$

calculator.description: Divide the gradient by the acceleration due to gravity.

steps.title: Identify physical relationship
steps.content:
To find the coefficient of static friction $\mu$, we first recognize that for an object on a rotating turntable, the centripetal force ($F_c$) required to keep it moving in a circle is provided by the static friction force ($F_f$). 

At the point of slipping, the maximum static friction is equal to the centripetal force. This concept is found in **Topic A.2.4 Circular motion**.

steps.title: Linearize the relationship
steps.content:
Using Newton's Second Law and the circular motion equations from the data booklet, we equate the friction force to the centripetal force:

$$\mu mg = m\omega_c^2 r$$

Rearranging this to solve for the angular velocity $\omega_c$:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega_c^2 &= \frac{\mu g}{r} \\ \omega_c &= \sqrt{\mu g} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{r}} \end{aligned}$$

This follows the linear form $y = mx$, where $\omega_c$ is on the $y$-axis and $\frac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$ is on the $x$-axis.

steps.title: Relate gradient to friction
steps.content:
By plotting $\omega_c$ against $\frac{1}{\sqrt{r}}$, the gradient ($k$) of the resulting straight line is given by:

$$\text{gradient} (k) = \sqrt{\mu g}$$

To find the coefficient of friction $\mu$, we rearrange this expression:

$$\begin{aligned} k^2 &= \mu g \\ \mu &= \frac{k^2}{g} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
From the experimental data, the gradient of the linearized relationship is approximately $3.11$. Using the acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (as typically used in this experiment context), we calculate $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{(3.11)^2}{9.81}$$

$$\mu \approx 0.99$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.11^2 / 9.81 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of friction by squaring the gradient and dividing by g.

steps.title: Establish the physics relationship
steps.content:
To find the coefficient of static friction $\mu$, we first relate the forces acting on the object at the point of slipping. The centripetal force required for circular motion is provided by the static friction force.

From the data booklet, the centripetal acceleration is:
$$a = \omega^2 r$$

According to Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$), the centripetal force is $m\omega^2 r$. At the critical angular velocity $\omega_c$, this equals the maximum static friction force $\mu mg$:
$$m \omega_c^2 r = \mu mg$$

This simplifies to:
$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{\mu g}{r}$$

This relationship is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**, specifically **A.2.4 Circular motion**.

steps.title: Rearrange for friction coefficient
steps.content:
We need to solve for $\mu$. Rearranging the equation from Step 0, we get:
$$\mu = \frac{\omega_c^2 r}{g}$$

Since we have a linearized graph of $\omega_c^2$ vs $\frac{1}{r}$, we can pick a single representative point from our line of best fit to substitute into this equation.

steps.title: Select a representative point
steps.content:
From the line of best fit drawn on your graph, identify a point $(\frac{1}{r}, \omega_c^2)$. 

Looking at our table, a clear point on the line is likely near $r = 0.10 \text{ m}$ where $\omega_c = 9.9 \text{ rad/s}$. 

Let's use these values for our calculation:
- $r = 0.10 \text{ m}$
- $\omega_c^2 = (9.9)^2 = 98.01 \text{ rad}^2 \text{ s}^{-2}$

calculator.description: Calculate the square of the critical angular velocity for the chosen point.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the formula using $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (or $9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ as per the constants table):

$$\mu = \frac{98.01 \times 0.10}{9.81}$$

$$\mu \approx 0.999$$

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of friction.

steps.title: State the final estimate
steps.content:
Rounding to two significant figures, as is appropriate for the data provided:

$$\mu \approx 1.0$$ (or $0.99$ depending on the exact placement of your line).

According to the markscheme, any value around $0.99$ based on the gradient of your line of best fit will earn full marks. Using a point directly from the line is a valid way to estimate this value.

parts.content: Suggest two assumptions or limitations in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- Assumes turntable rotates uniformly without vibration. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Write an expression for the total kinetic energy of the disc in terms of its translational and rotational motion.
parts.markscheme:
- State the expression for total kinetic energy: $E_k = \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the motion types
steps.content:
When a disc rolls without slipping, it undergoes two types of motion simultaneously:
1. **Translational motion**: The center of mass of the disc moves along the ramp at a velocity $v$.
2. **Rotational motion**: The disc spins around its center of mass with an angular velocity $\omega$.

Because energy is a scalar quantity, the total kinetic energy ($E_k$) is simply the sum of the kinetic energies associated with these two motions. This is a key concept in **Topic A.4 (Rigid Body Mechanics)**.

steps.title: Sum the energy components
steps.content:
To find the total kinetic energy, we combine the standard formula for translational kinetic energy with the formula for rotational kinetic energy:

- **Translational Kinetic Energy**: $E_{k, \text{trans}} = \frac{1}{2}Mv^2$
- **Rotational Kinetic Energy**: $E_{k, \text{rot}} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$

Adding these together gives us the expression for the total kinetic energy:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$

highlights.text: Write an expression for the total kinetic energy of the disc

steps.title: Identify motion types
steps.content:
When a disc rolls down a ramp, it undergoes two types of motion simultaneously:
1. **Translational motion**: The center of mass moves along the ramp with a velocity $v$.
2. **Rotational motion**: The disc spins around its center of mass with an angular velocity $\omega$.

Because of this, the total kinetic energy is the sum of the energy from its linear movement and its rotation. This concept is covered in **Topic A.4: Rigid body mechanics**.

steps.title: Sum the energy components
steps.content:
The general expression for the total kinetic energy ($E_k$) of a rolling object is:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$

Where:
- $M$ is the mass of the disc.
- $v$ is the translational velocity.
- $I$ is the moment of inertia.
- $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

steps.title: Substitute disc-specific inertia
steps.content:
To make this expression specific to a solid disc, we use the formula for the moment of inertia of a disc:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$

Substituting this into our total energy equation:

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}MR^2\right)\omega^2 \\ E_k &= \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{4}MR^2\omega^2 \end{aligned}$$

This represents the total kinetic energy in terms of the disc's physical properties and its motion.

parts.content: Calculate the moment of inertia of the disc about its central axis. Use $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the formula: $I = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4.0 \cdot (0.25)^2 = 0.125\ \text{kg m}^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To calculate the moment of inertia, we first identify the mass ($M$) and the radius ($R$) from the problem description. This question relates to **Topic A.4.2: Moment of inertia**, which is an HL-only topic.

- Mass, $M = 4.0\text{ kg}$
- Radius, $R = 0.25\text{ m}$

steps.title: Apply the substitution method
steps.content:
We use the specific formula provided in the question for a disc. We will substitute the known values directly into this equation:

$$I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$$

Substituting the values:
$$I = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times (0.25)^2$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Now, we perform the calculation. 

1. Square the radius: $(0.25)^2 = 0.0625$
2. Multiply by the mass and the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$:
$$I = 0.5 \times 4.0 \times 0.0625 = 0.125$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression in a new cell.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The moment of inertia is $0.125\text{ kg m}^2$. 

In an IB exam, you earn the **A1** mark for providing the correct value with the appropriate units. Ensure you always include the units ($\text{kg m}^2$) for rotational quantities.

parts.content: Determine the speed of the disc after descending a vertical height of $2.0\ \text{m}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use conservation of energy: $mgh = \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $\omega = \frac{v}{R}$ and values ($g=9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$):
$4.0 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2.0 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4.0 \cdot v^2 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.125 \cdot \left( \frac{v}{0.25} \right)^2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify and solve for $v$:
$78.48 = 2.0v^2 + 1.0v^2 = 3.0v^2$
$v = \sqrt{\frac{78.48}{3.0}} = 5.11\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: State energy conservation principle
steps.content:
As the disc rolls down, its initial gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at the top is converted into both translational kinetic energy ($E_{k}$) and rotational kinetic energy ($E_{rot}$) at the bottom.

Using the conservation of energy principle:
$$\Delta E_p = E_{k, \text{trans}} + E_{k, \text{rot}}$$

Substituting the standard formulas from the data booklet:
$$Mgh = \frac{1}{2}Mv^2 + \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$$

steps.title: Link linear and angular motion
steps.content:
Since the disc rolls without slipping, we use the rolling condition to relate the angular velocity ($\omega$) to the linear speed ($v$):

$$\omega = \frac{v}{R}$$

We also need the moment of inertia for a disc, $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$. Calculating this value first:
$$I = \frac{1}{2}(4.0)(0.25)^2 = 0.125\ \text{kg m}^2$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify equation
steps.content:
Now, substitute all known values into the energy equation ($g = 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$):

$$\begin{aligned} (4.0)(9.81)(2.0) &= \frac{1}{2}(4.0)v^2 + \frac{1}{2}(0.125)\left(\frac{v}{0.25}\right)^2 \\ 78.48 &= 2.0v^2 + \frac{1}{2}(0.125)\left(\frac{v^2}{0.0625}\right) \\ 78.48 &= 2.0v^2 + 1.0v^2 \\ 78.48 &= 3.0v^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for final speed
steps.content:
To find the speed, divide by $3.0$ and take the square root of both sides:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{78.48}{3.0}}$$
$$v = 5.11\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the square root of the quotient 78.48/3.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square root of (78.48 divided by 3).

calculator.description: Calculate the final speed from the energy conservation result.

steps.title: Analyze translational motion
steps.content:
First, we consider the forces acting parallel to the inclined plane. The component of the gravitational force pulling the disc down is $mg \sin(\theta)$, and a static friction force $f$ acts up the ramp to prevent slipping. Applying Newton's Second Law for translation:

$$mg \sin(\theta) - f = ma$$

Where:
- $m = 4.0\text{ kg}$
- $\theta = 30^\circ$
- $a$ is the linear acceleration

steps.title: Relate torque and rotation
steps.content:
The friction force $f$ provides the torque $\tau$ that causes the disc to rotate. We use the rotational version of Newton's Second Law: $\tau = I\alpha$.

Since it rolls without slipping, the angular acceleration $\alpha$ is related to the linear acceleration $a$ by $\alpha = \frac{a}{R}$. The torque is $\tau = fR$. For a disc, the moment of inertia is $I = \frac{1}{2}mR^2$.

Substituting these into the torque equation:
$$fR = \left(\frac{1}{2}mR^2\right) \left(\frac{a}{R}\right)$$
$$f = \frac{1}{2}ma$$

steps.title: Calculate linear acceleration
steps.content:
Now, substitute the expression for friction ($f = \frac{1}{2}ma$) back into the translation equation from Step 0:

$$\begin{aligned} mg \sin(\theta) - \frac{1}{2}ma &= ma \\ mg \sin(30^\circ) &= \frac{3}{2}ma \\ a &= \frac{2}{3}g \sin(30^\circ) \end{aligned}$$

Using $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ and $\sin(30^\circ) = 0.5$:
$$a = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.5 = 3.27\text{ m s}^{-2}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the linear acceleration using the derived formula.

steps.title: Find distance along slope
steps.content:
To use kinematics, we need the distance $s$ travelled along the ramp. We are given the vertical height $h = 2.0\text{ m}$. Using trigonometry:

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{h}{s} \implies s = \frac{h}{\sin(30^\circ)}$$

$$s = \frac{2.0}{0.5} = 4.0\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Determine final speed
steps.content:
Now apply the kinematic equation for an object starting from rest ($u = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned} v^2 &= u^2 + 2as \\ v^2 &= 0 + 2 \cdot 3.27 \cdot 4.0 \\ v^2 &= 26.16 \end{aligned}$$

$$v = \sqrt{26.16} \approx 5.11\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Solve for v by taking the square root of 2as.

parts.content: Explain why rolling without slipping allows mechanical energy to be conserved.
parts.markscheme:
- State that friction does no work because the point of contact is instantaneously at rest (no relative motion) <marks>R1</marks>

- Conclude that no mechanical energy is converted to thermal energy, so gravitational potential energy converts entirely into kinetic energy <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the point of contact
steps.content: When a disc **rolls without slipping**, the point of the disc in contact with the ramp is instantaneously at rest relative to the ramp. This means that at any given moment, the specific point on the rim where friction acts has a velocity of zero.

steps.title: Calculate work done by friction
steps.content:
In physics, work is done when a force causes a displacement. From the data booklet, the formula for work is:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Because the point of contact is instantaneously at rest, its displacement ($s$) at the exact moment the static friction force is applied is zero. Therefore, the work done by the friction force on the disc is zero ($W = 0$).

steps.title: Relate work to energy conservation
steps.content: Since static friction does no work, no mechanical energy is dissipated or converted into thermal energy (heat). This means the total mechanical energy of the system remains constant. As the disc moves down the ramp, its gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) is converted entirely into kinetic energy ($E_k$), including both translational and rotational components.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To summarize for the marks:
1. Friction does no work because the point of contact is instantaneously at rest (no relative motion).
2. Consequently, no mechanical energy is converted to thermal energy, allowing total mechanical energy to be conserved.
